output:
# ProjSWD: Correspondence-Aware Reprojection for Text-Driven NeRF Style Transfer, Evaluated on the 3D-WARP View-Consistency Benchmark

> **Method — ProjSWD:** a depth-and-pose-grounded cross-view *reprojection* consistency loss added to a Sliced-Wasserstein (SWD) stylization objective, enforced on a radiance field's *own* renders during fine-tuning.
> **Benchmark — 3D-WARP:** the first standardized warping-error view-consistency protocol for 3D style/edit methods, reported *jointly* with stylization strength to defeat blur-gaming.

---

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) [1] represent a scene as a continuous volumetric field optimized from posed images and rendered by differentiable volume rendering. Practical 3D pipelines now build on accelerated, modular backbones — Instant-NGP hash encodings [4], Mip-NeRF 360 anti-aliasing for unbounded scenes [3], and the Nerfstudio `nerfacto` model [2] — which make per-scene reconstruction a ~20–40 minute commodity operation. This work positions itself on the `nerfacto` backbone and inherits its render-and-fine-tune ergonomics.

**Text-driven 3D editing.** Instruct-NeRF2NeRF [6] edits scenes by iteratively *replacing* training images mid-optimization with InstructPix2Pix [5] edits (an Iterative Dataset Update), letting the field average out 2D disagreements over time. ViCA-NeRF [7] instead bakes consistency into the *supervision set before* optimization: it uses the NeRF's depth and poses to warp and blend already-edited 2D images so the training images are mutually consistent, then fits the field to them. Both rely on a diffusion image prior (often depth-conditioned via ControlNet [15]) and on style-aligned generation [11] for coherent multi-image style.

**Style transfer for radiance fields.** ARF [8] transfers a *single* reference image's style into a radiance field using a Nearest-Neighbor Feature Matching (NNFM) loss; StyleRF [9] performs zero-shot feature-space 3D style transfer; SNeRF [10] alternates 2D stylization and field updates. These are single-reference and **not text-driven**, deriving consistency implicitly from optimizing one shared field rather than from any explicit cross-view term.

**The SWD source method.** Classical neural texture/style work matches deep-feature *distributions*: Gatys et al. [14] via Gram matrices, AdaIN [13] via feature statistics, and — most relevant here — the Sliced Wasserstein loss [12], which matches VGG-19 feature distributions through many random 1-D projections. Our source pipeline stylizes each rendered view by minimizing SWD against a depth-conditioned, style-aligned diffusion target. SWD is perceptually strong but **per-view and global**: it matches *what* features appear, not *where* they land across views.

**Evaluation tooling.** Stylization strength is scored with CLIP and CLIP-directional similarity [18], perceptual quality with LPIPS [17], and 2D video stability with blind temporal-consistency warping error [16]. Notably, none of the 3D editing/style methods above report a *standardized geometric view-consistency* number — the void this work fills. The reprojection idea generalizes to other field representations such as 3D Gaussian Splatting [19].

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

**Core diagnosis.** SWD matches *global, per-view* feature statistics and contains **zero cross-view correspondence term**. Two renders of the same surface from different cameras can each independently satisfy the distribution-matching objective while disagreeing pixel-for-pixel on that surface. "Perceptual" agreement (matching histograms) is therefore **not** geometric consistency.

**Consequence.** Residual cross-view disagreements that SWD cannot see get **baked into the field** as the classic 3D-editing failure modes — ghosting, floaters, and blur — precisely the artifacts practitioners cite when they distrust NeRF edits. This is a structural gap in the objective, not a tuning problem.

**Evaluation void.** Instruct-NeRF2NeRF [6], ViCA-NeRF [7], ARF [8], StyleRF [9], and the SWD source all report stylization/edit quality but **none report a standardized geometric view-consistency metric**. The qualitative complaint ("it flickers") has no falsifiable number attached.

**The subtle trap.** Any naive view-consistency metric is **trivially gamed by blurring**: a blurred or under-stylized field has near-zero cross-view disagreement and therefore an excellent consistency score, while carrying no style. A consistency number reported *alone* rewards the degenerate solution. The only honest claim is a **joint** one — consistency *at a given stylization strength*.

## 3. Novel Insights and Recommendations

**Proposed direction.** Add a **correspondence-aware reprojection loss** that constrains the field's *own* renders: warp render *i* into view *j* using the field's rendered depth plus the known pose, and penalize the masked residual against render *j*. This is a closed geometric loop — the field's depth supervises the field's color — that directly attacks the residual disagreements SWD is blind to, with **no new architecture** (one additive loss term). Pair it with **3D-WARP**, a standardized warping-error benchmark, so the subfield finally shares a consistency yardstick across Instruct-NeRF2NeRF, ViCA-NeRF, ARF, StyleRF, and SWD-only.

**Concrete recommendations.**
- **Report a Pareto trade-off curve** (warping error vs CLIP stylization) by sweeping the consistency weight λ_cons; never accept a single consistency number — it is blur-gameable.
- **Evaluate with a FIXED external depth** (COLMAP MVS or GT), *not* the stylized field's own depth, to avoid circularity (a field rewarded for matching its own depth could improve the metric without improving the geometry).
- **Occlusion masking** via forward–backward round-trip checks, so disocclusion/silhouette residuals do not pollute the loss with garbage gradients.
- **Curriculum on λ_cons**: keep it at 0 for the first ~500–1k steps, then ramp, so the optimizer does not lock in an under-stylized blurry minimizer early.

## 4. Proposed Research Idea — The Five Questions (5q)

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**
Can a depth-and-pose-grounded cross-view reprojection consistency loss eliminate multi-view artifacts in text-driven NeRF style transfer without sacrificing stylization strength?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**
1. It targets the single correctness criterion that separates 3D editing from 2D — view-consistency. Ghosting/floaters/blur are the top reason practitioners distrust NeRF edits, and the SWD source has no cross-view term; the gap is closed with a cheap, principled loss rather than a new architecture.
2. It fills a real evaluation void: Instruct-NeRF2NeRF, ViCA-NeRF, ARF, StyleRF, and SWD report stylization/edit quality but none report a standardized geometric view-consistency number. A reprojection/warping-error protocol — model-agnostic, built from depth+pose the methods already have — turns a qualitative complaint into a falsifiable benchmark.
3. It is reusable and low-cost: the loss is one additive term and the metric is an eval script — no new representation and no diffusion retraining — so adoption cost is low (cf. the uptake of LPIPS and CLIP-directional).
4. It reshapes future research, shifting the question from "does it look stylized?" to "stylized *and* geometrically faithful across views?", enabling a Pareto (stylization vs consistency) view. The reprojection-regularization idea generalizes to any text-driven 3D editing that fine-tunes a field on per-view 2D supervision (instruction edits, relighting, inpainting), and the 3D-WARP metric scores 3D Gaussian Splatting edits [19] unchanged — a durable evaluation primitive.
5. It is practical: consistent, text-controllable 3D restyling serves VR/AR re-skinning, game/film set re-dressing, and digital-twin re-texturing — continuous-viewpoint settings where flicker/ghosting breaks immersion — so a measurable warping-error reduction has direct product value.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**
- **Naive per-pixel L2 over-smooths.** Stylized texture is *legitimately* view-dependent (brush strokes, speculars, anisotropic shading); a pixel-identity cross-view loss drives a blurry mean — high consistency, zero style — so the regularizer's easiest minimizer is exactly the failure mode.
- **SWD alone has no correspondence.** Global feature histograms can match while pixels disagree, so ghosting/floaters bake in unseen.
- **Chicken-and-egg depth drift.** The warp depth comes from the very field being stylized; as appearance changes, ray-termination depth shifts, so the correspondence keeps moving. Mitigated by warm-up curriculum, frozen/EMA geometry, and a fixed external depth for *evaluation*.
- **Occlusion masking is error-prone.** The round-trip thresholds τ_flow/τ_depth trade false-accepts against false-rejects, and the trade-off worsens at wide baselines.
- **View-dependent appearance is *correctly* inconsistent.** Speculars *should* differ across views; the loss must not penalize them, which requires a photometric/variance gate that is itself imperfect.
- **The λ_swd vs λ_cons balance IS the core trade-off.** There is no universal weight, forcing a sweep and a Pareto frontier rather than a single operating point.
- **The metric is trivially gamed by blurring.** A blurred field has ≈ zero warping error and no style, so warping error must be reported *jointly* with CLIP stylization, never alone; the only credible claim is a Pareto improvement over SWD-only.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**
The novelty is the *combination plus the metric*: this is the first method to enforce multi-view geometric correspondence INSIDE the fine-tuning objective of a text-driven, SWD-stylized NeRF — regularizing the field's OWN renders via depth-and-pose reprojection — paired with the first standardized warping-error view-consistency benchmark for 3D style/edit methods. No prior system fuses (1) text-guided depth-conditioned diffusion style targets [borrowed], (2) SWD distribution matching [borrowed from source], (3) explicit geometric reprojection consistency on the NeRF's own renders with forward–backward occlusion masking [new in this setting], and (4) a standardized quantitative view-consistency metric the subfield lacks [new as a shared protocol].
- **vs SWD-only source:** the source matches global sliced feature distributions per view, has ZERO cross-view geometric term, and reports NO consistency metric. Delta = add the correspondence-aware reprojection loss + quantify consistency; same pipeline, one new loss term + one metric; falsifiable.
- **vs Instruct-NeRF2NeRF [6]:** it iteratively *replaces* training images mid-optimization (Iterative DU) with no explicit correspondence objective and no consistency metric. We decouple/pre-compute editing and add a direct geometric reprojection term, removing unstable in-the-loop diffusion edits.
- **vs ViCA-NeRF [7] (sharpest):** ViCA uses NeRF depth+poses to WARP & BLEND already-edited 2D images in *preprocessing*, then fits the NeRF to those pre-consistent images — consistency is baked into the supervision set BEFORE optimization. Our delta = the consistency constraint lives INSIDE the fine-tuning objective, on the field's own renders, enforced in 3D DURING optimization — catching residual disagreements that survive 2D blending; it is orthogonal and composable with ViCA preprocessing.
- **vs ARF [8] & StyleRF [9]:** single-reference-image, non-text-driven (NNFM / feature-space). They get implicit consistency from optimizing one field but have no text control, no diffusion-prior style, and no explicit cross-view correspondence loss or metric. We are text-guided AND add the explicit reprojection term + benchmark.
- **vs 2D video temporal-consistency [16] & 3D-aware warping:** Lai et al. use optical-flow correspondences over a fixed external video to stabilize a 2D filter. We borrow the warping-error *idea* but our correspondences are the NeRF's own geometry (depth + known poses), and the regularized quantity is the same model producing that depth — a closed geometric loop in 3D, not post-hoc 2D smoothing.
- **Pre-empting "isn't this just a temporal-consistency loss on NeRF?":** No — (i) the source of correspondence is geometry (depth+pose) from the model itself, exact up to occlusion (forward–backward masking), not flow-estimation noise; (ii) what is constrained is a continuous radiance field's renders during optimization (a self-referential geometric loop where the field's depth supervises the field's color); (iii) the second prong is a standardized view-consistency benchmark enabling comparison across Instruct-NeRF2NeRF, ViCA-NeRF, ARF, StyleRF, and SWD — no analogue exists in 2D temporal-consistency work.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**
*Paradigm (unchanged):* stylize-in-advance, then fine-tune `nerfacto`; add a geometry-grounded consistency loss + standardized view-consistency protocol; no new architecture.
*Pipeline:* (a) reconstruct the source `nerfacto` from posed images + COLMAP; (b) render source RGB I_i and expected (ray-termination) depth D_i for every training pose, caching rays/poses T_i/intrinsics K — the warping substrate; (c) pre-generate style images offline via StyleAligned (shared-attention) [11] + ControlNet-depth [15] conditioned on D_i and the style text → stylized target S_i per view; (d) fine-tune `nerfacto` against S_i with the combined loss, rendering 256² patches to afford VGG features + view-pair warping.
*Objective:*

```
L = λ_swd · L_SWD  +  λ_cons · L_cons  [ + λ_nnfm · L_NNFM ]
```

- **L_SWD:** Sliced Wasserstein Distance between VGG-19 feature distributions (relu1_2, 2_2, 3_3, 4_3) of the rendered patch vs S_i; ~32–64 random projections, sort, L2 on the sorted projections, averaged over slices/layers (the source method's whole loss).
- **L_NNFM (optional):** ARF-style nearest-neighbor feature-matching content anchor at low, ablatable weight.
- **L_cons (NEW):** (1) render render_i, render_j and rendered depth D_i; (2) warp render_i → view j: backproject p_i with D_i to X = T_i·K⁻¹·[p_i;1]·D_i(p_i), then project to j via p_j = K·T_{i→j}·X with T_{i→j} = T_j⁻¹T_i, and bilinearly sample render_i → render_{i→j}; (3) build a forward–backward occlusion mask M by warping i→j→i and keeping pixels with round-trip error < τ_flow AND warped depth agreeing with D_j within τ_depth, dropping pixels that fail a photometric sanity gate (likely view-dependent/specular); (4) penalize the masked residual

```
L_cons = (1/|M|) · Σ_p  M(p) · ρ( Φ(render_{i→j})(p) − Φ(render_j)(p) )
```

  with Φ = identity (RGB) or VGG/LPIPS (preferred) and ρ = Charbonnier. View-pair sampling always uses 1 short-range pair + 1 long-range pair with prob ≈ 0.3. Curriculum: λ_cons = 0 for the first ~500–1k steps, then linearly ramp (avoids locking in an under-stylized blurry field, the degenerate minimizer).
*Datasets (why):* Mip-NeRF360 garden/bicycle/kitchen (360° unbounded, background floaters); Tanks&Temples Truck/Playground (large outdoor, wide baseline → long-range consistency); LLFF fern/flower/horns (forward-facing, self-occlusion → occlusion-mask test); Instruct-NeRF2NeRF scenes face/person/bear (apples-to-apples with the strongest text-edit baseline); ≥1 in-the-wild Nerfstudio capture (imperfect poses/exposure). The spread covers object-centric + 360 unbounded + forward-facing + large outdoor.
*Metrics (3D-WARP):* (i) **Warping error E_warp (PRIMARY)** — on HELD-OUT view pairs, reproject rendered view i into j via GT pose and a FIXED reference depth (COLMAP MVS or GT, NOT the stylized field's depth — avoids circularity), apply the occlusion mask, and take RMSE of the masked photometric residual (Lai-2018 style [16]); report short-range (adjacent) and long-range (wide-baseline) separately. (ii) Multi-view LPIPS consistency on the same masked warped pairs [17]. (iii) CLIP text-image + CLIP-directional similarity for stylization strength [18]. (iv) FID to the style domain (optional). (v) User study (n ≈ 20–30) pairwise A/B on rendered orbit videos (consistency + style fidelity). **Reporting rule:** plot the trade-off curve E_warp vs CLIP by sweeping λ_cons; success = a Pareto move (lower E_warp at equal-or-better CLIP than SWD-only); a single E_warp number is NOT accepted (blur-gaming).
*Baselines:* Instruct-NeRF2NeRF [6], ViCA-NeRF [7], ARF [8], StyleRF [9], and SWD-only source (= λ_cons = 0, the critical ablation).
*Ablations:* (1) feature/LPIPS-space vs RGB-space L_cons; (2) occlusion masking on/off; (3) short-only vs short+long pairs; (4) curriculum on/off; (5) depth-source sensitivity (NeRF-rendered vs COLMAP MVS vs GT) — attributing gains to the loss not the depth quality; (6) λ_cons sweep → trade-off curve.
*Compute:* source `nerfacto` ~20–40 min/scene on 1× RTX 3090/A6000; style pre-gen offline ~minutes/scene; stylization fine-tune with L_cons ~1.5–3 GPU-h/scene single-GPU (~1.3–1.6× over SWD-only); the full grid (~12 scenes × baselines/ablations) fits a single A6000 over days or a 4×-GPU node in ~1 day — single-GPU-scale by design.
*Expected outcome:* a **Pareto improvement** — strictly lower warping error at equal-or-better CLIP stylization than SWD-only — establishing that geometry-grounded reprojection regularization buys multi-view consistency without sacrificing style.

## 5. References and Evidence

[1] Mildenhall, B., Srinivasan, P. P., Tancik, M., Barron, J. T., Ramamoorthi, R., & Ng, R. (2020). NeRF: Representing Scenes as Neural Radiance Fields for View Synthesis. ECCV 2020.
[2] Tancik, M., Weber, E., Ng, E., Li, R., Yi, B., Kerr, J., Wang, T., Kristoffersen, A., Austin, J., Salahi, K., Ahuja, A., McAllister, D., & Kanazawa, A. (2023). Nerfstudio: A Modular Framework for Neural Radiance Field Development. SIGGRAPH 2023.
[3] Barron, J. T., Mildenhall, B., Verbin, D., Srinivasan, P. P., & Hedman, P. (2022). Mip-NeRF 360: Unbounded Anti-Aliased Neural Radiance Fields. CVPR 2022.
[4] Müller, T., Evans, A., Schied, C., & Keller, A. (2022). Instant Neural Graphics Primitives with a Multiresolution Hash Encoding (Instant-NGP). ACM TOG (SIGGRAPH) 2022.
[5] Brooks, T., Holynski, A., & Efros, A. A. (2023). InstructPix2Pix: Learning to Follow Image Editing Instructions. CVPR 2023.
[6] Haque, A., Tancik, M., Efros, A. A., Holynski, A., & Kanazawa, A. (2023). Instruct-NeRF2NeRF: Editing 3D Scenes with Instructions. ICCV 2023.
[7] Dong, J., & Wang, Y.-X. (2023). ViCA-NeRF: View-Consistency-Aware 3D Editing of Neural Radiance Fields. NeurIPS 2023. (arXiv:2402.00864.)
[8] Zhang, K., Kolkin, N., Bi, S., Luan, F., Xu, Z., Shechtman, E., & Snavely, N. (2022). ARF: Artistic Radiance Fields. ECCV 2022.
[9] Liu, K., Zhan, F., Chen, Y., Zhang, J., Yu, Y., El Saddik, A., Lu, S., & Xing, E. (2023). StyleRF: Zero-Shot 3D Style Transfer of Neural Radiance Fields. CVPR 2023.
[10] Nguyen-Phuoc, T., Liu, F., & Xiao, L. (2022). SNeRF: Stylized Neural Implicit Representations for 3D Scenes. ACM TOG (SIGGRAPH) 2022.
[11] Hertz, A., Voynov, A., Fruchter, S., & Cohen-Or, D. (2023). Style Aligned Image Generation via Shared Attention. arXiv:2312.02133 (StyleAligned).
[12] Heitz, E., Vanhoey, K., Chambon, T., & Belcour, L. (2021). A Sliced Wasserstein Loss for Neural Texture Synthesis. CVPR 2021.
[13] Huang, X., & Belongie, S. (2017). Arbitrary Style Transfer in Real-Time with Adaptive Instance Normalization (AdaIN). ICCV 2017.
[14] Gatys, L. A., Ecker, A. S., & Bethge, M. (2016). Image Style Transfer Using Convolutional Neural Networks. CVPR 2016.
[15] Zhang, L., Rao, A., & Agrawala, M. (2023). Adding Conditional Control to Text-to-Image Diffusion Models (ControlNet). ICCV 2023.
[16] Lai, W.-S., Huang, J.-B., Wang, O., Shechtman, E., Yumer, E., & Yang, M.-H. (2018). Learning Blind Video Temporal Consistency. ECCV 2018.
[17] Zhang, R., Isola, P., Efros, A. A., Shechtman, E., & Wang, O. (2018). The Unreasonable Effectiveness of Deep Features as a Perceptual Metric (LPIPS). CVPR 2018.
[18] Radford, A., Kim, J. W., Hallacy, C., Ramesh, A., Goh, G., Agarwal, S., Sastry, G., Askell, A., Mishkin, P., Clark, J., Krueger, G., & Sutskever, I. (2021). Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision (CLIP). ICML 2021.
[19] Kerbl, B., Kopanas, G., Leimkühler, T., & Drettakis, G. (2023). 3D Gaussian Splatting for Real-Time Radiance Field Rendering. ACM TOG (SIGGRAPH) 2023.
